context: Why Riot's actions are understandable, but why they might suffer in the long run. Riot's actions are not un-understandable. They want to build a moat and limit external influence. Retain a low risk/low threat environment for themselves while they continue reaping profits from the once in a lifetime super-lucky cash cow they obtained. They are probably even worried that LoL eSports might outgrow their company or become too 'big to handle' to some extent. If LoL esports becomes huge, it'll attract more and more big money competitors that will slowly eat away at the moat they're trying to build, and eventually compete and take away their profits. Riot currently has something akin to a monopoly on the MOBA space which currently is the most popular form of competitive gaming due to it's accessibility and simplicity for the common individual - DOTA is too complex to attract the average folks. Hence right now, like countless former short sighted companies in the past, they just want to maintain and reap their profits in comfort, avoiding large risks, large costs. The industry is super ripe for disruption. People are more than willing to have watching competitive games become a form of entertainment for them; even with LoL being a relatively simplistic game. Advertisers are also more than attracted enough to pay respectable amounts to those that are able to attract crowds via their superior skill and branding. With things like VR coming out, I expect some VR orientated game will eventually become super popular since players will feel they have more control over their characters for example. It's easy to see people tuning to watch the best VR players in the world fighting it out in some virtual environment. Point is: If Riot doesn't do it, someone else eventually will - that's what history says at least. There's enough money there for enough people to try (although global appitite for risky ventures has been in lows for many years). If someone published a decent enough Pokemon MOBA with some cool features, or a Anime universe MOBA consisting of popular characters from the anime universe, I'd switch anytime of the day for sure. LoL characters and setting is alright, but I'd always prefer many alternatives. [b]These competitors would recognise the signalling strength of having a strong associated esports industry, hence they'd be willing to give their teams more freedom to grow and spread - more freedom historically shows, more competition & consequently better results (build stronger brands, retain more fans, attract new players...).[/b] Sadly LoL still retains it's dominance currently - hence they are fine with having low risk preferences and having one sided relationships with eSports teams - even though, they might find that when they desire to do so out of necessity (other competitors attracting playerbase attention), it might be too late and their cash cow sinks into the graveyard of dead companies..

context: Glyphosate treated corn and soybeans almost all go to animal feed and in the case of corn to methanol production. EPSPS is not present in animals, which instead obtain aromatic amino acids from their diet. So unless you are a human plant, it is not very dangerous to you. Most crops don't even get sprayed with glyphosate because it kills them. Actually it is one of the lower danger pesticides to humans, but has a lot more press than the others. They are testing for residues of the pesticides that are more likely to harm humans. context: I don't really believe that it was America single handed lot fighting Germany. That was a joke. However, Japan was bombed because causalty estimates for a mainland invasion were in the millions. It was deeply ingrained in Japanese culture that it was better to die than bear the shame of surrender and capture. Between kamikaze fighters, banzai charges and how not a single japanese garrison had surrendered thoughout the entire war, how do you think a mainland invasion would go? It would have been a goddamn bloodbath. The Japanese were training everyone, military or civilian, to fight the American invasion that they were expecting. So the decision was made instead to drop the atomic bombs. ~250,000 people died from those blasts, and it was the better outcome than what would have been millions of deaths from an invasion. So was it unfair? Absolutely. Did it prevent hundreds of thousands of deaths? Yes.
